video_title: Carmen Li's Plan to Build a Futures Market for Compute | Odd Lots
gist: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange (backed by DRW's Don Wilson), details her plan to financialize GPU compute: Silicon Data has partnered with CME to launch CFTC-pending, cash-settled GPU futures and options within a couple of months, built on GPU price indices that normalize ~1M daily price points from ~200 data sources. She argues compute can become an oil-like hedging market and shares granular data on GPU rental rates, chip residual values, and the bubble debate.

insights.claim: Silicon Data has partnered with the CME to launch GPU futures and options within a couple of months, pending CFTC approval, making it among the first exchange-listed compute derivatives.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: A live, regulated GPU derivatives market on CME would be a tradable new asset class; watch for the CFTC approval date as the catalyst for compute hedging desks to open.
insights.quote: We will be launching GPU fusion options IMC at me in a couple of months pending CFTC approval obviously.

insights.claim: The CME GPU futures will be cash-settled against an index price like traditional oil contracts, not physically delivered, removing the need for buyers to take delivery of chips.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Cash settlement means traders can hedge or speculate on compute prices without operational infrastructure, lowering the barrier for financial participants to enter the market.
insights.quote: For the CME, futures will be financially settled. Just like, the traditional oil settlement price

insights.claim: GPU futures will be tradable through any existing commodities/futures broker using the same workflow and margin process as semis products, giving institutions cross-margin optimization with no new infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Frictionless access via existing CME pipes lowers adoption cost for banks and trading desks already clearing commodities, accelerating potential liquidity.
insights.quote: The beauty of CME is you can do the same thing you're doing now trading semi products okay. The same process and processing margin.

insights.claim: Silicon Data launched what it calls the world's first GPU price indices on the Bloomberg Terminal in 2025 after roughly two and a half years of development beginning in 2023.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Establishes Silicon Data as an incumbent benchmark provider; a Bloomberg-distributed index gives it a distribution edge as compute derivatives scale.

insights.claim: Silicon Data's GPU index is built by collecting roughly six months of historical training data from over 100 sources, processing over 150,000 traded prices daily and normalizing them to a base case rather than using a simple average, to account for differing CPU, RAM, memory bandwidth and location.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: The normalization approach is the moat: an index that genuinely tracks the price users pay neoclouds is what makes hedging viable, so methodology rigor differentiates it from competing indices.
insights.quote: We usually collect six months of historical training data from over 100 data sources, and we see which factor drives the price differentiation.

insights.claim: Silicon Data now ingests around one million pricing points globally from approximately 200 data sources via individual licensing agreements with neoclouds, hyperscalers and marketplaces.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: The breadth of licensed data is a defensible barrier to entry; new index competitors must replicate ~200 bilateral data relationships to match coverage.
insights.quote: Right now, with a million pricing points globally, around 200 data sources, it's pretty much BAU.

insights.claim: Normalized daily volatility for A100 and H100 indices runs about 20-30%, a healthy commodity range, whereas individual unnormalized chip configurations at specific locations show 80% to over 100% volatility.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Volatility in the healthy commodity range validates the existence of a hedging market; the gap between raw and normalized vol shows the index itself is what makes the product tradable.
insights.quote: The daily volatility for A100 H100 is around 20 to 30. There's a very healthy commodity volatility range.

insights.claim: Neoclouds and entities holding GPUs on their balance sheet are 'naturally long' compute and should short futures to stabilize revenue, while everyone who consumes GPUs is 'naturally short' and should buy futures to cap cost volatility, mirroring oil producers versus consumers in WTI/Brent.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Maps the natural buyer/seller base for compute derivatives; neoclouds with GPUs on balance sheet are the structural 'shorts' that give the market two-sided flow.
insights.quote: You have GPUs right. Or the banks where GPUs on your balance sheet right. Your long GPUs. Then naturally you want to make sure your revenue right is stable

insights.claim: On-demand GPU prices can swing from roughly $3 to $6 to $9 per GPU-hour depending on the supply-demand curve, pushing buyers from on-demand toward forward reserve contracts to lock in predictable margins and guaranteed capacity.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: The 3x price swing quantifies the cost volatility that creates hedging demand; the shift to forward contracts signals the buyer base is already seeking price certainty.
insights.quote: Your price can go from $3 to 6 to $9. Depends on demand supply curve shifting.

insights.claim: Banks and speculators have been in discussions with Silicon Data and some can internally cross both sides of the compute trade, but Carmen Li actively encourages institutions to stand up dedicated compute trading desks because GPUs are not homogeneous and require hardware, use-case and software-layer expertise.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Speculator participation is still nascent; the gating factor for liquidity is specialized desks, so the emergence of dedicated compute trading teams is the signal that the market is maturing.
insights.quote: I selfishly I want them to start trading desk on compute. The more people trade, the better for me

insights.claim: Silicon Data published a paper (with Jefferson Lab at the GPGPU conference) proving 38% performance variance for the same A100 80GB chip, decomposed into chip-level, intra-provider and inter-provider variance, meaning identical-spec GPUs are not interchangeable.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: The 38% performance dispersion is the core fungibility problem for compute as a commodity; it justifies independent benchmarking and is a risk for index-based settlement (basis risk).
insights.quote: We prove there's 38% performance variance for the same chip, and then we decompose into the chip self, intra provider and inter provider.

insights.claim: Compute Exchange independently verifies GPU performance (FLOPS, memory bandwidth, tokens) before delivery via a platform Li likens to a 'Carfax for GPUs,' letting buyers trade off price against location, latency and verified performance.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Verified-performance procurement addresses the buyer-trust gap in spot compute; transparency tooling is a differentiator that could underpin physically-settled compute contracts later.
insights.quote: We in compute change you actually verify the GPU before delivered to you.

insights.claim: A100 and H100 use cases are currently not well correlated and serve fairly separate workloads, but improving software, model compression and optimization could make legacy chips substitutable for newer ones within months, shifting the supply-demand curve.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Cross-chip substitutability driven by software is a key swing factor for relative GPU pricing; traders must watch the software/optimization layer, not just hardware supply.
insights.quote: As the software layer compression model compression gets better, optimization gets better, things can change.

insights.claim: H100 on-demand neocloud rental rates fell through last year but have risen roughly 8% over the last three months, and even the older A100 has risen about 10-15% over the same period, signaling a tightening supply-demand curve after a period of declining prices.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Rising rates on both current and legacy chips contradicts the 'compute prices always fall' narrative and points to renewed scarcity; a bullish signal for GPU owners' revenue.
insights.quote: I think the last three months came up to like 8% for H100.

insights.claim: Carmen Li notes that on the prior Odd Lots episode (October) compute prices were falling across all chips, but she has since seen prices reverse upward, crediting Joe Weisenthal for calling the turn before the market did.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Confirms a regime change in compute pricing from late-2025 declines to 2026 increases, reframing GPU rental as a now-appreciating commodity.

insights.claim: Silicon Data's analysis (per Li's public blog) showed H100 second-year residual/resale value for refurbished chips holding around $0.85 on the dollar, with a notable drop from year one to year two but a flatter curve thereafter, far better than common 'rapid obsolescence' narratives.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Strong H100 residual values support depreciation assumptions used in datacenter cash-flow and credit underwriting; counters the bear thesis that chip obsolescence destroys asset value.
insights.quote: The second year, h100 residual value of resale value for refurbished shows about $0.85 on the dollar.

insights.claim: Compute Exchange runs a chip refurbishment/used-GPU business (likened to a 'Carvana for compute'), helping buyers run residual-value and break-even cash-flow models comparing newest chips like the B300/GB-series against cheaper refurbished H100s.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: A functioning secondary market for used GPUs underpins residual value assumptions and creates an alternative supply channel that could cap new-chip pricing power.

themes.theme: Inference market breadth and the bubble question

insights.claim: Silicon Data tracks pricing and consumption across 300 open-source, open-weights and closed-source models, and its consumption-weighted LLM token price index roughly doubled to about $21 per million tokens (blended input/output) from December 1 of the prior year.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: A doubling of consumption-weighted token prices challenges the assumption that inference costs only fall; signals shifting demand toward higher-value models and a new tradable token benchmark.
insights.quote: Right now the price actually doubled file indices found out from December 1st last year. It's like $2 $21 per million token

insights.claim: Li frames AI bubble risk at the machine level as a simple test of whether discounted future cash flows from forward GPU contracts repay the price paid, and argues bringing GPUs online is slow (waitlists, co-location, optical fiber) so multi-billion-dollar datacenter announcements do not translate to immediate supply.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Provides a discounted-cash-flow framework to assess GPU asset valuations; the supply-deployment lag argues against an imminent oversupply crash even amid large capex announcements.
insights.quote: Look at the future cash flow of your forward contracts and then you discount it back. Can you give money back for the price you pay?

insights.claim: Li warns that contract defaults are the unhedgeable counterparty risk in compute (defaults can hit even long-term signed contracts and a wave of built-out supply could collapse prices via oversupply), but says GPU cost/price risk itself is precisely what CME futures are designed to hedge, and CDS-style instruments could emerge.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Identifies counterparty default and oversupply as the key tail risks in the compute economy; flags potential demand for compute credit-default products as the market deepens.
insights.quote: The things you cannot hedge is GPU cost right. The price you interned. So that's something exactly what CME futures for.

insights.claim: Silicon Data already tested compute price contracts on Polymarket (settling contracts in February and April) before going exclusive with CME, and Li welcomes prediction-market GPU contracts as on-ramps rather than threats since users will gravitate to whichever benchmark best supports hedging.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Shows multiple venues experimenting with compute price contracts; the durable edge is a hedge-quality, hard-to-manipulate index rather than the trading venue itself.
insights.quote: We actually work with Poly Market. Last year someone actually list my product at Poly Market

insights.claim: Silicon Data is no longer the only GPU price index provider, and Li argues the market will reward indices that are hard to manipulate and genuinely usable for hedging over those that merely enable speculation.
insights.speaker: Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data and Compute Exchange
insights.so_what: Index competition is intensifying; the winning benchmark will be the one liquidity coalesces around, so watch which index CME-style hedgers actually adopt.
insights.quote: The market always vault while sourcing. Start trading the market. Always gravitate towards things. Actually help them hedge
